actions.thought: 64o is a very weak starting hand even in 3-handed play. Although I have position as the Button, playing trash hands is a quick way to lose chips, especially unsuited, disconnected low cards. I will fold and wait for better spots.

54 offsuit is a weak starting hand with very little equity. Since I am in the small blind and will be playing out of position against the big blind, folding is the most disciplined and profitable long-term decision to preserve my stack.

I am holding pocket Queens, which is a premium starting hand. Being on the button, I want to capitalize on my position and build the pot. Since no one has raised yet, an open-raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard and effective strategy to thin the field and extract value from weaker hands.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Deuce offsuit. The Button (Flashlight) has opened with a 3x raise. Q2o is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Even against a wide Button opening range, this hand does not meet the criteria for a call or a 3-bet bluff. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

K9o in the big blind facing a 3x raise from the button. While it's a hand with some high-card value, K9o plays poorly out of position and suffers from reverse implied odds against a button open. Folding is the standard and safest play to conserve chips early in the tournament.

I am on the button with Jack-Five offsuit (J5o). In a three-handed game, the button should open a wide range of hands to put pressure on the blinds. However, J5o is significantly below the standard opening range even for a three-handed tournament. It has poor playability post-flop and doesn't dominate many hands that would call a raise. Given it is early in the tournament (Hand 3 of 150) and stacks are deep (100 BBs), there is no need to take unnecessary risks with such a weak hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

5s 9c is a very weak, disconnected offsuit hand. Even from the small blind with only 50 to call, the lack of post-flop playability and showdown value makes folding the clear choice. We don't want to get involved out of position against the Big Blind with trash.
Q6 offsuit is a weak hand and not strong enough to open from the Button 3-handed. Folding to wait for better opportunities.

I am in the small blind heads-up against the big blind after the button folded. With suited connectors like JTs, I have a strong enough hand to play aggressively. Raising to 300 is a standard approach to steal the blinds or build a pot, as this hand has good playability post-flop.

KTh is a premium suited broadway hand, especially in a 3-handed game. With the Button folding and the Small Blind (Flashlight) raising to 300, I am facing a wide range of hands. I will 3-bet to 900 to extract value, gain initiative, and potentially end the hand pre-flop while I have a strong hand.

I raised to 300 from the small blind and was 3-bet to 900 by the big blind. With suited connectors like JTs, I have good equity against a standard 3-bet range, even though I am out of position. 4-betting is risky early in the tournament without a premium hand, and folding is too tight given the pot odds and my hand's potential. Calling is the most strategic play, allowing me to see a flop and realize my hand's value while keeping the pot manageable.

I have middle pair (Jack) on a board of Ks-2h-Jc. Being out of position against the pre-flop 3-bettor, it is prudent to check. Betting here is unlikely to generate value against better hands and risks playing a large pot with a marginal holding. Checking allows me to re-evaluate based on my opponent's action while keeping the pot size manageable.

I have top pair with a Ten kicker on a King-high board. Having 3-bet pre-flop, I have the range advantage on this texture. Flashlight checked to me, so I will place a continuation bet of 600 (1/3 pot) to extract value from Jack-x hands, middle pairs, or gutshots like Q10, while maintaining control of the pot.

I have middle pair (Jack) on a K-J-2 board. The opponent's bet is 600 into an 1800 pot, giving me good pot odds to continue. Given I have middle pair with a backdoor spade draw, folding is too conservative, and raising would likely only isolate me against stronger hands that have me beat. Calling allows me to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the turn.

I am in a 3-bet pot and out of position. The turn card (Qs) has significantly improved my hand, giving me an open-ended straight draw and a flush draw. With such strong equity, checking is the most prudent play to control the pot size, allow me to re-evaluate based on my opponent's action, and potentially realize my equity for free if they check back.

The turn Queen is a significant card that interacts well with Flashlight's calling range (hands like QJ, KQ, or even AT). While I still have top pair, my kicker (Ten) is now less effective, and the board is highly coordinated. Checking back allows me to control the size of the pot and see a river for free, where I might improve to a straight or safely reach showdown.

The board is K-2-J-Q-4. My hand is a pair of Jacks. With the turn checked through, my hand functions primarily as a bluff-catcher. Given that I am first to act on the river, checking allows me to reach a showdown for free while avoiding potential value bets from stronger hands like a King or Queen. Betting is unlikely to be profitable as it would likely only be called by better hands.

On the river 4c, the board remains very coordinated (K-Q-J-2-4). Flashlight has checked for a second time after calling my flop continuation bet. While I have top pair with Kings, the presence of the Queen and Jack on the board means many hands in Flashlight's calling range (like QJ, KJ, KQ, or even AT/T9) now beat me. By checking back, I control the pot and ensure I reach showdown with a hand that still has significant value against missed draws or weaker Jacks, without risking a check-raise from a superior hand.

Holding 5-8 offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand with poor playability, especially in a 3-handed game. There is no strategic value in opening this hand from the button, so folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with Jd 9h. Flashlight has folded on the button, leaving just me and the Big Blind (Pro). J9o is a reasonable hand to open from the small blind in a short-handed game to put pressure on the big blind. I will raise to 300 to attempt to take down the blinds now or play with the initiative post-flop.
62 offsuit is one of the worst hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the small blind, defending is out of the question. Folding to preserve chips.

I am on the button with Kc 3c, which is a suited King and strong enough to open for a raise in a three-handed game. Opening here allows me to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds or play post-flop with positional advantage. I will raise to a standard 3x size.

Facing a 3bb raise from the Button, A8o is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. Calling leaves me vulnerable to a squeeze from the Big Blind and puts me in a tough post-flop spot. A 3-bet is possible as a bluff, but folding is the most solid and standard decision with an offsuit weak Ace.

Holding 3h 5c in the Big Blind is a clear fold. The hand has very little equity, is offsuit, and disconnected. Calling 200 to see a flop out of position against a button raiser with such a weak holding is not profitable. I will conserve my chips for a more favorable situation.

Pocket sevens is a strong hand to open from the button in a 3-handed game. Raising to 250 (2.5x the big blind) is a standard open that puts pressure on the blinds to fold, allows me to take the initiative, and keeps the pot manageable if I face a 3-bet or see a flop.

I am holding a very weak hand, 5-2 offsuit, which has poor playability. Being in the Small Blind and out of position against a button raise, there is no positive expected value in defending or calling. Folding is the disciplined and optimal play to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with 10-4 suited. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250. With 100 already committed as the big blind, it costs me 150 more to call into a pot of 400. This gives me pot odds of approximately 2.6:1. In a three-handed game, 10-4 suited is a reasonable hand to defend against a Button opening range given these odds. I will call and see a flop, playing cautiously out of position.

I have a gutshot straight draw (needs a 7) and a backdoor diamond flush draw on an 8-9-5 board. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Pro), the standard play is to check to him and see how he proceeds. Leading into him here doesn't accomplish much with my current hand strength. I will check and evaluate his bet size if he chooses to continue.

With pocket sevens on an 8s-9c-5d board, I have a vulnerable underpair to the two top cards, but I also have a gutshot straight draw to a 6. This connected board favors the big blind's defending range, which contains many combinations of 9x, 8x, straight draws like JT, 76, and two-pairs. Betting here doesn't get called by many worse hands and opens me up to a check-raise, forcing me to fold my equity. Checking behind controls the pot size, allows me to realize my equity (hitting a 6 for a straight or a 7 for a set), and lets me evaluate the turn for cheap.

On the turn, the board is 8s-9c-5d-6h, which is extremely coordinated. I hold Td-4d, giving me a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7). Since I am out of position and the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) checked back on the flop, he may have a marginal hand or is looking to control the pot. I will check here to see if he checks back again, allowing me a free card to hit my straight, or to see how he reacts to the turn card before committing more chips with only ten-high.

The turn card is the 6h, giving me a 9-high straight (5-6-7-8-9). The board is completely rainbow, meaning there are no flush draws to worry about. The only hand that beats me is T7 for a ten-high straight, which is heavily blocked by my pocket sevens. Since Flash checked again, I need to start building the pot and extracting value from hands like two pair, sets, strong top pairs, or worse straights (like a bare 7). Betting around two-thirds of the pot gets value from the big blind's wide defending range.

On the turn, the board is 8s-9c-5d-6h. I have Td-4d, which gives me a gutshot straight draw (needing any 7 to make a 10-high straight). Pro bet 350 into a 900 pot after checking the flop. This bet represents roughly 40% of the pot. To call, I need approximately 22% equity. With only 4 outs (the 7s) to make my straight, I have about 8.7% equity to hit on the river. Given that the board is extremely coordinated and a 7 on the river would put a four-straight on the board (5-6-7-8-9), my implied odds are likely low because it will be difficult to get paid off. Since I lack the proper pot odds and the implied odds aren't strong enough to compensate, I will fold.

I am on the button with 7-2 suited, which is a very weak starting hand. Even though I have position, playing such a hand is generally unprofitable in the long run. I prefer to fold and wait for better opportunities to enter the pot.

With the button folding, I am in the Small Blind with 7-6 offsuit. In a blind-vs-blind situation, this hand is strong enough to attempt an open-raise to steal the blinds or take initiative in the hand. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard sizing that puts pressure on the Big Blind while maintaining a balanced opening range. If called, I will have to play out of position, but the connectivity of 7-6 allows for some board coverage on various textures.

Facing a 3x open from the Small Blind, defending Jack-Nine suited from the Big Blind is a standard play. We have position post-flop, the hand has good playability, and we are getting a reasonable price to see a flop.

I am the pre-flop aggressor in a blind-vs-blind battle. The flop is Kd-4s-5h, giving me an open-ended straight draw (3 or 8). This is a dry board that favors my perceived range (which includes many Kx hands). I will place a continuation bet of 300, which is 50% of the pot, to represent strength and potentially take the pot down now or build equity for my draw if called.

We missed the flop completely with Jack-Nine suited, catching only a backdoor flush draw. Facing a half-pot continuation bet on a King-high board from the pre-flop raiser, we have virtually no equity and no viable bluffing opportunities right now. Folding is the standard and most profitable play.

I am on the button with 53o, which is a very weak starting hand. Even in a short-handed (3-player) game, 53o lacks enough equity or playability to justify an open raise from the button. Folding is the standard disciplined play here to preserve my chip lead.

With QTo in the Small Blind and the action folded to me, I have a strong enough hand to open-raise against the Big Blind. Raising to 300 (3x) puts pressure on the Big Blind, allows me to win the pot immediately a good portion of the time, and defines the range if called, compensating for being out of position post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 3d-2d, which is a very weak hand. Defending the big blind with such low suited connectors out of position against an aggressive opponent like Pro is negative expected value. Given the stack depth and the strength of the hand, folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack.
I am dealt 36 offsuit on the button, which is a bottom-tier starting hand. There is no reason to put any chips in the pot with this holding. I will simply fold and wait for a better opportunity.

With Ace-Deuce offsuit in the small blind and the button having folded, I am heads-up against the big blind. While A-2o is not a premium hand, it has reasonable showdown value in a heads-up pot. Completing the small blind (calling the additional 50) allows me to see a flop cheaply while maintaining a controlled pot size, especially given that I will be out of position for the rest of the hand.

I am in the Big Blind with KJo. The button folded and the Small Blind (Flashlight) just completed/called the blind. KJo is a very strong hand in a blind-vs-blind scenario and is significantly ahead of a typical small blind limping range. I will raise to 400 to take control of the pot, extract value, and potentially end the hand immediately.

I am in the small blind holding Ace-Deuce offsuit. After completing the small blind, the big blind raised to 400. A-2o is a weak hand that is often dominated by the big blind's raising range. Playing this hand out of position in a raised pot is high variance and likely to lead to a difficult situation post-flop. Given the stack depths and the aggressive play observed, folding is the most disciplined and profitable decision to preserve my stack for a better spot.

Q8o is a marginal hand that performs poorly in 3-handed play against opponents who have shown a tendency to play aggressively. Given my stack size is the shortest at the table, preserving chips while waiting for a better opportunity is the most disciplined professional approach.

I am in the Small Blind with 73s. The button has folded. While I have been aggressive in previous hands and successfully stolen blinds, 73s is a very weak hand to play out of position against 'Pro' in the Big Blind. Raising here might invite a defense or a 3-bet from an observant opponent. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

I am on the button with Jack-Trey suited. While this hand has some potential due to being suited and in position, J3s is generally at the bottom of or just outside a standard opening range for a 3-handed game. Given that I am currently the chip leader and have seen some aggression from the 'Pro' player in earlier hands, I will opt for a disciplined fold here rather than opening with a marginal hand that is easily dominated.

With 4d2d in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I have a very weak hand out of position against the Big Blind. It's best to fold and preserve my chips for better spots rather than attempting a low-equity steal or limping into a likely disadvantageous post-flop situation.
With Td 7h offsuit from the button, my hand is too weak to open the action, especially with full stacks. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

Holding 6d 7s is a weak hand with low equity. Given my position in the small blind and the fact that I am facing a big blind opponent with a larger stack, there is no strategic advantage to completing the call with such a marginal holding. Folding minimizes further risk to my stack in an unpromising situation.

Jack-four offsuit is an extremely weak hand with poor equity. Even in a three-handed game on the button, there is no strategic justification for investing chips into this pot given the low potential for post-flop playability. Folding is the standard and disciplined choice.

In a blind-on-blind situation after the button has folded, Qh 3h is a hand with enough equity and post-flop playability to warrant a raise. Attacking the Big Blind's range here allows me to take down the blinds pre-flop or navigate the hand with the initiative. A standard 3x raise is appropriate.
J5o is a very weak, disconnected offsuit hand. Facing a 3x raise from the small blind, there is no justification for defending. Folding is the most profitable action.

Holding 4-6 offsuit on the button in a three-handed game. While the button usually allows for a wider opening range, 4-6o is at the very bottom of that range and is not worth a raise into two active opponents. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve chips for better spots.
Folded to me in the small blind. I hold 83o, which is a very weak starting hand and well outside any standard opening range. I will simply fold.
Jack-Eight offsuit is too weak of a hand to open from the button, even with fold equity against the blinds. Folding is the standard play here to conserve chips for better starting hands.

I hold a premium hand (AKo) in the Small Blind position after the Button folded. Raising is the optimal play to take initiative and build a pot against the Big Blind. A raise to 300 chips (3x the big blind) is a standard opening size that puts pressure on the opponent while being committed to a strong hand.

I am in the Big Blind with a pocket pair of Threes (3d, 3s). Flashlight raised to 300 from the Small Blind after Pro folded. With a pocket pair, I have a hand that is likely ahead of many Small Blind opening ranges, and even if I'm behind, the pot odds (calling 200 more into a 400 pot) make it very profitable to see a flop and attempt to set-mine or win at showdown. I will call to see the flop.

I hold top pair with the nut kicker (Ace-King) on a board of Ah-Qh-9s. With a flush draw present and potential straight draws, I need to bet for value and protection. A bet of 350 chips represents a standard continuation bet size of slightly over half the pot, allowing me to build value against weaker Aces or Queens while charging any drawing hands.

I have a small pocket pair (3d, 3s) on a board of Ah-Qh-9s. This board is very dangerous as it connects well with the Small Blind's pre-flop raising range (containing many Aces, Queens, and Broadway cards). Flashlight has led out with a bet of 350 into a 600 pot. My hand has very little equity and limited potential for improvement. Continuing would likely be throwing chips away, so I will fold.

I am in the button position with 3s 2h, which is an extremely weak starting hand. There is no reason to invest chips here against two opponents. Folding is the standard and correct strategic play to preserve my stack.

In this three-handed game, I am in the Small Blind with Qd9h. The Button (Flashlight) has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Pro) in the hand. Q9o is a strong enough hand to raise for value and to attempt to take the pot down immediately from the Small Blind against a single opponent. A standard raise to 3x the Big Blind (300 total) is appropriate here to exert pressure and build a pot with a hand that has reasonable equity if called.

Flash is opening to 3x from the small blind, which is typically a very wide range attempting to steal the big blind. AJo is a premium hand in a blind-vs-blind scenario. By 3-betting, I can extract value from worse hands like weaker Aces or broadways, take the betting initiative, and often win the pot uncontested pre-flop. A sizing of 900 (3x the raise) is standard and puts adequate pressure on his wide opening range.

I opened from the Small Blind with Q9o, which is a standard play, but the Big Blind (Pro) has responded with a 3-bet to 900. While I have to call 600 more to see a flop, Q9o is a weak hand to play out of position against a 3-bet. Pro's range here likely consists of strong Broadway hands and pairs that dominate me. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of deuces. This is a standard opening hand in a 3-handed game. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds or play for set value post-flop.

Facing a 3bb open from the Button while in the Small Blind. A3o is too weak and plays poorly out of position to call, and is not a premium candidate for a 3-bet bluff compared to suited Ax hands. Folding is the standard and highest EV play.

I hold pocket eights, a strong hand that plays well against an aggressive button opener's range. Since I am out of position in the big blind, I want to take the initiative and thin the field or potentially take down the pot pre-flop. A 3-bet to 1000 is a standard size that allows me to define the hand strength and discourage light calls, while keeping my stack healthy if the opponent chooses to continue.

I am on the button with pocket deuces and raised to 300. The Big Blind (Flashlight) has 3-bet to 1,000. While I have position, pocket deuces are the weakest pocket pair and are primarily played for set value. To call the additional 700, I would need to be confident in my implied odds. Flashlight's remaining stack is approximately 6,650 after the 3-bet. The 700 call represents more than 10% of the effective stack, which makes set-mining mathematically marginal (the 'Rule of 15' suggests needing at least 15 times the call amount in effective stacks). Furthermore, if I miss the set, I will almost certainly have to fold to any continuation bet on the flop. Given the tournament context and the strength shown by a large 3-bet, folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

I have KTs on the Button in a 3-handed game. This is a premium starting hand that plays very well post-flop and dominates many hands in the blinds' calling ranges. I will open-raise to 2.5x (250) to build the pot, take control of the hand, and potentially steal the blinds or play a bloated pot in position.

I hold Five of Spades and Ten of Diamonds, which is a very weak, offsuit, and disconnected starting hand. Being in the Small Blind, I am out of position for the remainder of the hand against the Button (Pro). Calling would commit 200 chips into a situation where I will likely have to play fit-or-fold on the flop without initiative. Given the lack of strength of my hand and the disadvantage of being out of position, the correct play is to fold and conserve my stack for a better opportunity.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h6h, a suited one-gapper that plays reasonably well post-flop. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250, and the Small Blind has folded. I need to call 150 more to see a flop, and the pot is currently 400. This gives me pot odds of 2.67-to-1. Given the strength of my hand relative to a typical Button opening range and the favorable price, defending my blind by calling is the most standard and mathematically sound play. I will call to see the flop out of position.

I have bottom pair on a very wet board (8s-Qs-9s). The board is monotone with three spades, and I do not hold a spade myself. This board texture is likely to have hit the Button's opening range well, or at least provides them with many semi-bluffing opportunities. I will check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how they react and to control the size of the pot with a marginal hand.

On a monotone 8s Qs 9s board, I hold King-Ten with no spades. I only have a gutshot straight draw, and any flush is already complete or has strong draws. Without a spade in my hand, betting as a bluff or for value is unprofitable against a Big Blind calling range that connects well with this board. Checking behind to see a free turn is the most sensible play.

The board has four spades (8s, Qs, 9s, Ts), creating a four-flush. I hold 8h 6h, giving me a pair of eights, but I do not have a spade. Any single spade in the opponent's hand makes a flush. Additionally, the Ten of Spades completes various straight possibilities. Since the flop went check-check, it's possible Pro is weak, but the board is far too dangerous to bet into without a spade or a high straight. I will check to see how the opponent reacts to this extremely wet board.

The board now has four spades (8s Qs 9s Ts), and I hold Kh Th, giving me no spades. Any single spade in my opponent's hand beats me, making my hand virtually worthless. Since my opponent checked to me, taking a free card by checking behind is the only sensible play. Betting here would serve no purpose, as I would only get called or raised by a spade.

On a board with four spades (8s Qs 9s Ts) and a potential straight, my pair of eights (8h 6h) has very little showdown value. However, since the flop and turn both checked through, it's possible my opponent is also weak. I will check to see if I can get to a cheap showdown. Betting would likely only get called by hands that beat me (any spade, any Jack, or a higher pair).

With four spades on the board (8s Qs 9s Ts) and no spade in my hand, my best five-card hand is a pair of tens with a king kicker (using the board). My opponent has checked all three streets, suggesting they likely don't have a strong spade, but they might still hold a weak spade, an Ace, or a Jack that they would call a bet with. Betting here has zero value and would only serve as a bluff, which is unnecessary and risky given my marginal showdown value against other non-spade holdings. Taking the free showdown is the optimal play.

Q8 offsuit is a relatively weak hand, even in a three-handed game. My opponents have shown tendencies to play aggressively, making it risky to open with a marginal hand from the button. It is more prudent to wait for a better opportunity to enter the pot.

I am in the Small Blind with 5s 4h, which is a very weak hand. Since the Button has folded, it is just me and the Big Blind (Pro). While I could attempt to steal the blinds with a raise, 54o is near the bottom of my range and plays poorly out of position if called. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my chip lead.

I am on the button with 8h 5s. While I have the opportunity to open the pot and try to steal the blinds, 85 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability post-flop. Given that I have been relatively active in recent hands, I will choose to fold here to maintain a balanced image and avoid committing chips with a sub-standard hand in a three-handed game.

With K4o in the small blind and the button having folded, I have a weak offsuit King. While stealing from the small blind is a valid strategy with a decent range, K4o is generally too weak to open-raise with, and limping leaves me out of position with a marginal hand that plays poorly post-flop. The most standard and profitable long-term play here is to simply fold and preserve my stack.

J5 suited is too weak of a hand to open from the button even in a 3-handed game. I don't want to get involved with marginal holdings when I can easily fold and wait for better opportunities to exploit my opponents or steal the blinds.

I am in the small blind with Ace-Deuce offsuit. The button (Pro) has folded, leaving me heads-up against the Big Blind (Flash). Given the pot odds, I am getting 2:1 to call (50 to win a 100 pot). Completing the blind is a standard, low-risk way to play this hand in a heads-up situation, allowing me to see a flop cheaply with an Ace-high hand while avoiding inflating the pot with a weak kicker.

I am in the big blind with 7d 3d. The button has folded and the small blind (Flashlight) has limped in. Since I have a weak hand and have already committed my blind, the best play is to check and take a free flop rather than bloating the pot with a sub-par hand.

The board texture 5c-4h-9c is relatively dry but connects well with a Big Blind's calling range, which contains many of these middle cards. My hand, Ace-Deuce, has one overcard and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 3). Betting here would likely be a bluff that gets called by any pair, so checking is the standard play to control the pot size and realize my equity or fold to a significant bet if I don't improve on the turn.

In this limped pot, I have a very weak hand (7-high) with only a backdoor straight draw and a gutshot to the six. Since the Small Blind has checked to me, checking back is the most sensible play to see a free turn card. There is no need to risk chips by bluffing on such a dry board in a small pot.

I am in the small blind with Ace-Deuce on a board of 5c-4h-9c-Qs. The flop was checked through, indicating neither player has a strong made hand. With Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw to the wheel (needing a 3 to make a straight), I have some equity, but not enough to justify betting against a range that likely includes many pairs or made hands that will not fold. Checking allows me to see the river card for free, controlling the pot size, and attempting to realize my equity without committing more chips with a marginal hand.

Flashlight has checked both the flop and the turn in this limped pot, suggesting a weak range. While I could attempt to steal the pot with a bet, I have zero showdown value and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 6). Checking behind allows me to see the river for free and potentially realize my equity if I hit the straight, without risking chips on a bluff in a low-stakes pot.

The board is 5c-4h-9c-Qs-9d. Both players checked the flop and turn, indicating neither has a strong made hand. My Ace-high is unlikely to win against a pair, but there is no value in betting as any call would beat me. Checking is the only logical play to take the hand to showdown without risking further chips.

I have 7-high and no showdown value. However, the pot is very small (2 BBs) and Flashlight has checked every street after limping. While a bluff might work against their weak range, the reward is minimal compared to the risk of being called by a better high-card hand or a small pair. I will check and concede the pot if I am beaten.

I am on the button with 7-4 offsuit, which is an extremely weak hand with no playability. Entering the pot with this holding is statistically unfavorable. Therefore, I will fold and wait for a better hand.

In a blind-vs-blind situation after the Button folds, I have T8o which is strong enough to open-raise. Pro in the Big Blind has shown a pattern of folding to pre-flop pressure in previous hands. Raising to 300 allows me to potentially take the blinds immediately or play the hand with initiative if called.

QJs is a premium suited hand that plays exceptionally well in position. Facing a 3x open from the small blind, defending by calling is a standard and highly profitable play, allowing us to leverage our positional advantage post-flop while keeping the opponent's range wide.

I hold the 8 of spades, providing a flush draw on a monotone flop. As the pre-flop aggressor in a blind-versus-blind battle, a continuation bet is standard to maintain initiative and fold out many of the Big Blind's hands that do not contain a spade. A bet of 300 (half the pot) is a balanced sizing that applies pressure without over-committing.

The flop is monotone with three spades (4s 7s 2s), and I hold Qh Jh with no spades. Without a flush draw, backdoor flush draw, or any piece of the board, my overcards have very little equity. Facing a continuation bet from the small blind on this texture, the standard and most mathematically sound decision is to fold.

I am on the button with Ten-Deuce offsuit (T2o), which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Out of position in a 3-way pot with an underpair to the board. Checking to the pre-flop raiser is the standard approach here. The board is relatively dry, but checking allows me to control the size of the pot and evaluate the actions of my opponents, keeping my options open if a bet is made.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Four offsuit, which is a weak hand. Playing out of position against a button raise with such a marginal holding is generally unprofitable. Folding is the most disciplined and standard strategy here.

Checking might induce a bluff, but betting ensures I extract value from marginal hands that would otherwise check behind. I am in the small blind with a weak hand (10-3 offsuit). Folding is the disciplined play here to avoid playing a poor hand out of position post-flop, especially given the low potential of 10-3o to hit favorable boards. I will concede the small blind already posted.

6h 5h is a playable suited connector, especially on the button. With a 50BB stack, playing aggressively in position is key to accumulating chips and stealing the blinds. Opening to 300 is a standard raise to take initiative in the hand and put pressure on the small and big blinds.

I have an overpair (Kings) on a highly coordinated board. The 8d on the turn is a dangerous card as it completes straights for any hand containing a five (like 55, 65s, or 75s). I must call to realize my equity and contest the showdown.

I am on the button with 83 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Even in a three-handed game, 83o is not part of a standard opening range from the button. Given the tournament context and the need to preserve my stack, there is no reason to play this hand. I will fold.

With A5 offsuit in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I have a decent starting hand with an Ace blocker. Raising here is a standard steal attempt to take down the blinds immediately or take control of the pot going into the flop against the Big Blind.

42s is a very weak one-gapper suited hand. From the button 3-handed, it's far too weak to open-raise profitably, even as a steal, and calling is definitely unprofitable. Folding is the standard and correct decision.

I am on the button with a pocket pair (3d, 3h), which is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. My goal is to take control of the hand and use my positional advantage. I am in the Small Blind with A8o. Flashlight (Button) has raised to 300. A8o is a strong hand in 3-handed play, particularly against a button open which typically has a wide range. Facing a raise from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind, my 2s4c is a trivial fold. It is one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em and has terrible playability post-flop, especially out of position in a 3-bet pot.

I am holding a pocket pair of threes on the button, which has good set-mining potential and playability post-flop. Flash, the small blind, has 3-bet me to 1000. Folding would be too conservative given my position and the strength of the pair, while 4-betting would turn my hand into a bluff against what is likely a strong range. Calling 700 more to see a flop is the most balanced approach, allowing me to realize my equity and potentially outplay the opponent if I flop a set or if the board texture allows for a continuation bet later.

I was the pre-flop 3-better, which gives me a range advantage on this board. A dry, paired board like 7-7-2 is excellent for a continuation bet. My Ace-high likely has the best hand against some of the button's calling range, but I want to deny equity to cards like overcards or flush draws. A 1/3 pot bet (700) is standard here to maintain pressure and clarify where I stand.

Flash is betting 700 into a 2150 pot on a 7-7-2 board. My pocket threes are ahead of his 3-betting range, which likely contains many overcards (e.g., AK, AQ, AJ) and potentially some broadway hands. Raising would likely only get called by hands that beat me (like an overpair) or force him to fold hands I am currently dominating. Calling allows me to control the pot size and realize my equity, keeping him in with hands I beat while maintaining a manageable stack for future streets. The pot odds are favorable, making this a clear call.

The Queen of Hearts on the turn is a significant scare card. It completes potential flush draws and is a card that could have hit the opponent's calling range if they held hands like AQ or KQ. My Ace-high still has some showdown value, but it's unlikely to be the best hand if I face significant aggression now. Checking allows me to see how the opponent reacts, control the pot size, and potentially reach a cheaper showdown if they check back with a medium pair or a draw.

Flash checked the turn on a 7-7-2-Q board. Checking back allows me to realize my showdown equity cheaply and avoid bloating the pot against a potentially trapping opponent.

On this double-paired board (7-7-Q-Q), I currently hold two pair, Queens and Sevens, with an Ace kicker (Qs-Qh-7s-7h-As). My opponent's line (calling a 3-bet pre-flop, calling a flop bet on a dry board, and checking the turn) suggests they have either a medium pocket pair (88-JJ), a Seven, or a missed draw/Ace-high. Since I only beat hands that have no pair higher than a 7, betting for value is risky as I'm unlikely to be called by worse. Checking is the most prudent play to reach a cheap showdown and realize my equity against their Ace-high hands or missed draws.

The river board of 7-7-2-Q-Q does not improve my pocket threes, which are now a bluff-catcher that loses to any pair higher than threes, including any Queen or Seven. Checking back is the most optimal way to realize my showdown equity.

King-Ten offsuit is a strong enough starting hand to open from the button in a 3-handed game. I will raise to 2.5x the big blind to try and steal the blinds or take the lead into a post-flop pot where I will have position.

I am on the button with 2h 8h, which is a very weak starting hand with poor playability. In this 3-handed game, the pot is 150 (50 SB + 100 BB), and I am the first to act. There is no incentive to play such a weak hand, so I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am holding Seven-Deuce suited on the button. While being on the button allows for a wide opening range, 7-2 is the weakest hand in poker and even the flush potential does not make it a profitable open against active opponents in the blinds. I will fold to preserve my chip lead.

I am in the Small Blind with 6c Ts, a marginal offsuit hand. Playing out of position against the button's raise is not ideal. The hand lacks the equity and playability to warrant a call, especially when I can wait for a better spot to engage with a stronger holding.

I have bottom pair with no clubs on a very coordinated monotone board (9c Tc 4c). Checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard and most prudent play here to gauge their strength and minimize loss on a board that likely hits their opening range better than mine.

The board now shows four clubs (9c Tc 4c 7c). I hold Ad 4s, giving me a pair of fours but no club. Pro checked back on the flop, which could mean he lacks a club or a strong made hand, but betting here into a four-flush board without a club myself is extremely risky. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size, take a free card to potentially hit my straight flush on the river (with a 6c or Jc), and avoids being check-raised off my strong equity.

On a board with four clubs, any single club in my opponent's hand makes a flush. I have two pair (Sevens and Fours), but without a club in my hand, my hand is extremely vulnerable. I have completed an Ace-high straight (A-2-3-4-5) on the turn. However, the board of 2-3-4-5 is extremely coordinated, and any 6 in Flashlight's hand would make a higher straight (2-3-4-5-6). Calling allows me to keep the pot size manageable, see the river, and evaluate the final action based on the last card and Flashlight's behavior.

I missed my flush and straight draws on the river, leaving me with only Queen-high, which has virtually zero showdown value against an opponent who called my turn raise. Given that the board is paired and my opponent has shown strength by calling on the turn, a bluff bet here would be highly speculative and likely ineffective. On the river, the board has paired with a 3h, making the final board 2c-4c-3s-5h-3h. I hold an Ace-high straight (A-2-3-4-5). T2 suited is too weak to play, even from the button. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve my stack.

I am holding 3d 2c in the Small Blind, which is arguably the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the Button. Facing a 3x raise from the Button, King-Eight offsuit is too weak to defend from the Big Blind. Out of position, this hand suffers from significant reverse implied odds and poor playability. Folding is the best choice to preserve our stack.

I am on the button with 6s 2d, which is an extremely weak hand. Although I have the chip lead and positional advantage, this hand has almost no equity and poor playability. I have Ace-Four offsuit in the Big Blind. The Small Blind (Flashlight) raised to 300. A4o is a strong hand in a blind-vs-blind situation. I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker on a King-high board. Flashlight (the pre-flop raiser) checked. There's no reason to bet here as I likely won't get called by many worse hands, and checking back allows me to realize my equity and keep the pot small with a marginal hand.

I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker (Fours). The board is Ks-9h-4d-Js. Flashlight, the pre-flop aggressor, has checked both the flop and the turn. I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker (Fours). The board is Ks-9h-4d-Js-8c. Flashlight, the pre-flop aggressor, has checked all three streets (flop, turn, and river). K7 offsuit is a weak hand with poor playability, especially when facing aggressive opponents like Flash and Pro. Even from the button, there is no strong incentive to enter the pot with this holding.
